article.title: Copy Fail Linux Kernel Flaw Lets a 732-Byte Script Reach Root on Almost Every Distro Since 2017 as CISA Sets a May 15 Patch Deadline
article.summary: CVE-2026-31431 lets an unprivileged user gain root with a 732-byte Python script. CISA added it to KEV on May 1 and ordered federal agencies to patch by May 15.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A local privilege escalation flaw in the Linux kernel's cryptographic subsystem, dubbed Copy Fail and tracked as CVE-2026-31431, allows any unprivileged user on a vulnerable system to gain root with a 732-byte Python script, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/). The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency added the bug to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 1, 2026, and directed federal civilian agencies to apply fixes by May 15, [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html) reported.

## What We Know

Copy Fail carries a CVSS score of 7.8 and was discovered by the offensive security firm Theori, [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/) reported. According to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/), "Theori researcher Taeyang Lee identified the vulnerability, with the help of the company's AI security scanning software, Xint Code."

The flaw sits in the Linux kernel's `authencesn` cryptographic template, a component of the AF_ALG userspace crypto interface, [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/) reported. By chaining AF_ALG socket operations with the `splice()` system call, an unprivileged local user can perform a controlled four-byte write into the page cache of any readable file on the system, [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/) reported. Because the kernel reads the page cache when it loads a binary, that write effectively rewrites the on-disk binary at runtime without modifying the file on disk or tripping file-system monitoring tools, [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/) noted. Targeting a setuid binary such as `/usr/bin/su` is enough to escalate to root.

Theori summarized the primitive in a sentence quoted by [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/): "An unprivileged local user can write four controlled bytes into the page cache of any readable file on a Linux system, and use that to gain root." The same outlet describes the published proof of concept as "a 10-line, 732-byte Python script capable of editing a `setuid` binary to gain root on almost all Linux distributions released since 2017."

The finders explicitly compared Copy Fail with two earlier high-impact Linux flaws. [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/30/linux_cryptographic_code_flaw/) wrote that "Copy Fail is similar to other LPE bugs such as Dirty Cow and Dirty Pipe, but its finders claim it doesn't require winning a race condition and it's more broadly applicable." According to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/), the researchers framed the difference more bluntly: "Copy Fail is more portable. One script, every distro, no offsets."

Upstream fixes for the bug landed in the mainline kernel on April 1, 2026, and ship in kernel versions 6.18.22, 6.19.12, and 7.0, [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-linux-copy-fail-flaw-gives-hackers-root-on-major-distros/) reported. The same outlet noted that Theori built and tested its 100-percent-reliable Python exploit against Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, Amazon Linux 2023, RHEL 10.1, and SUSE 16. [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/new-linux-copy-fail-vulnerability.html) listed advisories from "AlmaLinux, Amazon Linux, Arch Linux, CloudLinux, Debian, Gentoo, Red Hat Enterprise Linux, SUSE, and Ubuntu."

## Cloud and Container Impact

Microsoft's security team published its own analysis of the flaw on May 1. The [Microsoft Security Blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/01/cve-2026-31431-copy-fail-vulnerability-enables-linux-root-privilege-escalation/) describes the underlying primitive as "A bug in the Linux kernel's crypto-subsystem can be abused by an attacker to corrupt the cache of any readable file, including setuid binaries." The same post warns that "because the page cache is shared across containers and the host, the vulnerability also enables cross-container impacts and container escape scenarios," and that "successful exploitation leads to full root privilege escalation (high impact to confidentiality, integrity, and availability) and could facilitate container breakout, multi-tenant compromise, and lateral movement."

[The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/04/new-linux-copy-fail-vulnerability.html) quoted Bugcrowd founder David Brumley placing Copy Fail in the same family as earlier page-cache attacks: "Copy Fail is the same class of primitive, in a different subsystem." The same article relayed the Theori team's own four-property summary of why this bug stands out: "This vulnerability is unique because it has four properties that almost never appear together: it's portable, tiny, stealthy, and cross-container."

## Federal Response

CISA added CVE-2026-31431 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 1, 2026, [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html) reported. The same article noted that the catalog entry describes a flaw in which "Linux Kernel contains an incorrect resource transfer between spheres vulnerability," and that "Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies have been advised to apply the fixes by May 15, 2026." [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/cisa-adds-actively-exploited-linux-root.html) characterized the bug as a "nine-year-old flaw" and noted that it "is also tracked as Copy Fail by Theori and Xint."

## What We Don't Know

Neither the CISA listing nor the published advisories disclose where the in-the-wild exploitation that triggered the KEV addition was first observed, who is exploiting the bug, or against which targets. The mainline patch landed on April 1, but rollout pace across cloud-hosted Linux fleets, hardened embedded systems, and long-tail enterprise kernels is uneven and not centrally tracked. The bug was introduced in 2017, leaving roughly nine years of intermediate kernel releases in scope, and it is not yet clear whether anyone exploited Copy Fail before its public disclosure on April 30.

## Why It Matters

The combination of a tiny, deterministic exploit, a vulnerable code path that has been present in the kernel for nearly a decade, and a primitive that crosses container boundaries puts Copy Fail in the small group of Linux flaws that demand same-week patching across server fleets. CISA's May 15 deadline gives federal agencies two weeks; the wider Linux ecosystem will spend longer working through the long tail.
